refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain end-to-end teleoperation communication latency with p95 ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions to enable safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-008 addresses a critical safety and operational requirement. Teleoperation latency directly impacts operator situational awareness, control responsiveness, and safety margins. The p95 percentile metric ensures consistent performance under variable network conditions rather than optimistic best-case assumptions. High safety criticality demands explicit latency budgeting across the communication chain.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure end-to-end latency (sensor capture → network transmission → operator display) under representative network conditions (agreed bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profiles). Collect p95 percentile over extended operational windows. Validate against 150 ms threshold during acceptance testing with Safety Manager and Tele-operator participation.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: Sensor data acquisition and local processing shall introduce ≤ 30 ms latency
FR-NGHE-008.2: Network transmission of telemetry shall achieve ≤ 80 ms latency under agreed conditions
FR-NGHE-008.3: Operator interface shall render telemetry and accept control input with ≤ 40 ms latency
FR-NGHE-008.4: System shall monitor and report latency health to Safety Services for anomaly detection

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement distributed latency budgeting with hardware-accelerated sensor-to-network pipeline, optimized codec selection, and QoS-prioritized network routing
DP-B: Deploy edge computing node co-located with vehicle sensors to pre-process and compress telemetry before network transmission, reducing payload and latency variance
DP-C: Establish dedicated low-latency network channel with reserved bandwidth and priority queuing; implement local telemetry buffering and predictive rendering on operator side
DP-D: Combine DP-A + DP-C: latency budgeting + dedicated network channel with QoS enforcement and operator-side predictive rendering
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement comprehensive latency architecture combining: (1) explicit latency budgeting across sensor acquisition, network transmission, and operator rendering; (2) dedicated low-latency network channel with QoS-prioritized routing and reserved bandwidth; (3) hardware-accelerated sensor pipeline with optimized codec; (4) operator-side predictive rendering and control buffering; (5) continuous latency monitoring and anomaly reporting to Safety Services.

potential_sources: DP-D couples Teleoperation module (network channel, codec, rendering) with Vehicle module (sensor pipeline, hardware acceleration) and Safety Services (latency monitoring, anomaly alerts). Coupling is intentional and necessary—latency is a system-level property requiring coordination across domains.
Risk: Tight coupling between network QoS assumptions and vehicle sensor timing. Mitigation: explicit interface contracts on Telemetry and Network Link interfaces; latency SLA documentation.
coupling_check.notes: DP-D is not decoupled but is appropriately coupled. The latency requirement is inherently cross-module; attempting to isolate it to a single module would violate the functional intent. Coupling is managed through explicit interface specifications and monitoring.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Network infrastructure supports QoS prioritization and reserved bandwidth allocation under agreed conditions.
Assumption: Vehicle hardware (sensors, processors) can sustain ≤ 30 ms acquisition-to-transmission pipeline.
Assumption: Operator display hardware and software can render telemetry and accept input within ≤ 40 ms.
Risk: Network conditions may degrade beyond agreed profile (congestion, packet loss spike). Mitigation: Safety Services monitors latency; operator receives SafetyAlert if p95 exceeds threshold.
Risk: Predictive rendering may diverge from actual vehicle state if control commands are delayed. Mitigation: continuous state reconciliation; operator training on latency-aware control techniques.
Risk: Hardware acceleration (codec, sensor pipeline) may introduce non-deterministic latency spikes. Mitigation: extensive testing under load; latency profiling and jitter characterization.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: DP-D is sound but should be refined to explicitly separate concerns: (1) Vehicle-side latency budget (sensor → network interface), (2) Network-side latency budget (transmission), (3) Operator-side latency budget (reception → display). Each sub-DP should have clear ownership and measurable targets. Add explicit fallback strategy: if latency exceeds threshold, system shall degrade gracefully (e.g., reduce telemetry frequency, increase operator control authority limits, trigger Safety Alert).

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Owns end-to-end latency architecture including: network channel management, QoS enforcement, codec selection and optimization, operator-side rendering pipeline, control input buffering, and latency monitoring. Interfaces with Vehicle (via Telemetry and Tasking) and Safety Services (via SafetyAlert and latency health reporting).

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives sensor data from Vehicle (position, orientation, camera feeds, proximity sensors, etc.). Teleoperation module buffers, compresses, and transmits over Network Link. Latency budget: ≤ 30 ms from Vehicle sensor capture to Telemetry interface handoff.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Sends operator control commands (movement, tool actuation, mode changes) to Vehicle. Teleoperation module accepts operator input, buffers, and transmits via Network Link. Latency budget: ≤ 40 ms from operator input to Tasking interface delivery.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional network transport for Telemetry (inbound) and Tasking (outbound). Teleoperation module manages QoS, prioritization, and reserved bandwidth. Latency budget: ≤ 80 ms round-trip under agreed network conditions.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Reports latency anomalies (p95 exceeds 150 ms, jitter spike, packet loss) to Safety Services. Enables proactive safety response (operator notification, control authority reduction, emergency stop readiness).

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of latency architecture. Codec, rendering, control buffering, QoS management, and operator-side monitoring are core teleoperation concerns. Minimal diffusion: only Telemetry and Tasking interface contracts leak to Vehicle; Network Link is external infrastructure.

Vehicle owns sensor acquisition and local processing (≤ 30 ms budget), but latency optimization (codec, buffering, QoS) belongs in Teleoperation. If Vehicle owns full latency architecture, it becomes tightly coupled to network and operator concerns, violating separation of concerns. High diffusion risk.

Safety Services monitors latency health and triggers alerts, but does not own the latency architecture itself. Assigning latency DP to Safety Services would scatter implementation across modules and obscure accountability. Safety Services consumes SafetyAlert from Teleoperation.

NGHE Solution is the system-level container but is too coarse-grained for implementation. Latency architecture requires dedicated module with clear ownership, interfaces, and testing strategy. Assigning to NGHE Solution would defer critical design decisions.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of DP-D (latency architecture). It naturally encapsulates codec, rendering, control buffering, QoS management, and operator-side monitoring. Vehicle module owns sensor acquisition and local processing (sub-FR-008.1) but delegates network and operator concerns to Teleoperation. Safety Services consumes latency health via SafetyAlert interface.
containment_risks: Risk: Teleoperation module may become large and complex if it also owns operator UI, tele-presence features, and multi-operator coordination. Mitigation: clearly scope Teleoperation to latency-critical path; defer UI and coordination to separate modules if needed.

Risk: Vehicle module may resist latency budgeting constraints (e.g., sensor sampling rate, processing overhead). Mitigation: explicit interface contract on Telemetry (latency SLA); Vehicle team owns sensor-side budget; Teleoperation team owns network and operator-side budget.

Risk: Network Link is external infrastructure; Teleoperation cannot fully control latency if network is congested or degraded. Mitigation: Safety Services monitors and alerts; operator training on latency-aware operation; fallback strategies (reduced telemetry frequency, control authority limits).
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Teleoperation assignment is sound. Recommend: (1) Explicitly document latency budget allocation across Vehicle (≤ 30 ms), Network Link (≤ 80 ms), and Teleoperation operator-side (≤ 40 ms). (2) Define Telemetry and Tasking interface contracts with latency SLAs. (3) Establish latency monitoring and reporting mechanism (SafetyAlert). (4) Plan for graceful degradation if latency exceeds threshold. (5) Assign sub-FR-008.1 (sensor acquisition) to Vehicle; sub-FR-008.2 (network transmission) to Network Link (external); sub-FR-008.3 (operator rendering) to Teleoperation; sub-FR-008.4 (latency monitoring) to Teleoperation with Safety Services integration.

fr_dp_gate.reason: Refined FR-008 clearly specifies the latency requirement (p95 ≤ 150 ms) and safety context. DP-D (comprehensive latency architecture) directly addresses the FR through latency budgeting, QoS management, and monitoring. The mapping is clear and traceable. DP-D is not over-constrained; it allows flexibility in codec choice, network routing, and rendering strategy while ensuring latency targets are met.

diffusion_risks: Teleoperation module must coordinate with Vehicle (Telemetry interface) and Safety Services (SafetyAlert interface). Diffusion risk is low because interfaces are well-defined and latency ownership is clear.
Network Link is external infrastructure; Teleoperation cannot fully control it. Mitigation: explicit QoS contracts and monitoring.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation is the optimal container for DP-D. Codec, rendering, control buffering, and operator-side monitoring are core Teleoperation concerns. Vehicle owns sensor acquisition (sub-FR-008.1); Teleoperation owns network and operator-side latency (sub-FR-008.2, 008.3). Safety Services consumes latency health via SafetyAlert. Containment score 95/100 for Teleoperation; no better alternative.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Latency requirement is measurable and testable. End-to-end latency can be instrumented with timestamps at sensor capture, network transmission, and operator display. p95 percentile can be computed over extended test runs. Acceptance criteria are clear: p95 ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Latency budget is allocated across Vehicle (≤ 30 ms), Network Link (≤ 80 ms), and Teleoperation (≤ 40 ms). Each module has clear ownership and measurable targets. Teleoperation module is responsible for end-to-end latency architecture and monitoring.

recommended_revisions: Refine sub-FR-008.1 through 008.4 to include explicit latency targets and verification methods.
Document latency budget allocation in a system-level latency analysis document (SLAD) with traceability to FR-008.
Define Telemetry and Tasking interface contracts with latency SLAs and jitter bounds.
Establish latency monitoring and reporting mechanism in Teleoperation module; define SafetyAlert trigger thresholds.
Plan for graceful degradation strategies if latency exceeds threshold (e.g., reduced telemetry frequency, control authority limits, operator notification).
Conduct latency profiling and jitter characterization during design phase; validate with prototype testing.

decision.justification: FR-008 is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs (sensor acquisition, network transmission, operator rendering, latency monitoring). Each sub-FR requires further design and allocation. DP-D is sound and well-mapped to Teleoperation module, but implementation details (codec selection, QoS algorithm, rendering strategy, monitoring mechanism) must be refined at the next level. Proceed with functional decomposition of sub-FR-008.1 through 008.4 to establish detailed design parameters and implementation modules.
decision.justification_detail: The refined FR-008 is clear and safety-critical, but the DP-D (latency architecture) is a high-level strategy that requires decomposition. Sub-FRs address distinct concerns: (1) sensor pipeline (Vehicle domain), (2) network transmission (infrastructure domain), (3) operator rendering (Teleoperation domain), (4) latency monitoring (Safety Services integration). Each sub-FR should be decomposed to identify specific design parameters (e.g., codec type, QoS algorithm, rendering technique, monitoring interval) and implementation modules. Teleoperation module assignment is appropriate and stable; no revision needed at this level.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: Sensor data acquisition and local processing shall introduce ≤ 30 ms latency (decompose to sensor sampling, buffering, compression)
FR-NGHE-008.2: Network transmission of telemetry shall achieve ≤ 80 ms latency under agreed conditions (decompose to QoS routing, packet prioritization, error handling)
FR-NGHE-008.3: Operator interface shall render telemetry and accept control input with ≤ 40 ms latency (decompose to rendering pipeline, input buffering, predictive display)
FR-NGHE-008.4: System shall monitor and report latency health to Safety Services for anomaly detection (decompose to latency instrumentation, threshold detection, alert generation)

assumptions: Network infrastructure supports QoS prioritization and reserved bandwidth under agreed conditions.
Vehicle hardware (sensors, processors) can sustain ≤ 30 ms acquisition-to-transmission pipeline.
Operator display hardware and software can render telemetry and accept input within ≤ 40 ms.
Tele-operator is trained in latency-aware control techniques and understands predictive rendering behavior.
Safety Manager has approved latency thresholds and fallback strategies (e.g., control authority reduction, emergency stop).
Network Link interface provides deterministic latency bounds under agreed conditions (no congestion, packet loss within spec).
open_risks: Network conditions may degrade beyond agreed profile (congestion, packet loss spike). Mitigation: Safety Services monitors latency; operator receives SafetyAlert if p95 exceeds threshold.
Predictive rendering may diverge from actual vehicle state if control commands are delayed. Mitigation: continuous state reconciliation; operator training.
Hardware acceleration (codec, sensor pipeline) may introduce non-deterministic latency spikes. Mitigation: extensive testing under load; latency profiling.
Teleoperation module may become large and complex if it also owns operator UI and multi-operator coordination. Mitigation: clearly scope to latency-critical path.
Vehicle module may resist latency budgeting constraints. Mitigation: explicit interface contract on Telemetry with latency SLA.
evidence_needed: Latency profiling data from prototype testing (sensor capture to operator display, p95 percentile under agreed network conditions).
Network QoS capability assessment (reserved bandwidth, priority queuing, jitter bounds).
Operator display hardware and software latency characterization (rendering pipeline, input buffering).
Vehicle sensor and processor latency characterization (sampling rate, processing overhead).
Safety Manager approval of latency thresholds and fallback strategies.
Tele-operator feedback on latency-aware control techniques and predictive rendering usability.
System-level latency analysis document (SLAD) with budget allocation and traceability to FR-008.
